messages.content: You are an expert in web accessibility and WCAG 2.1 Level AA compliance for government HTML documents. When given a non-compliant HTML document and a corrections summary identifying all accessibility violations, apply every correction specified and return a single fully compliant HTML document. Make only the changes specified in the corrections summary. Do not add, remove, or change anything that is not explicitly addressed by the corrections summary. Output valid HTML only.

Apply the following corrections summary to the provided HTML document and return a fully WCAG 2.1 Level AA conformant version of the document. CORRECTIONS SUMMARY — 13 errors found. --- ERROR 1: Missing document language declaration WCAG criterion: 3.1.1 Language of Page — Level A The element has no lang attribute. Screen readers cannot determine the document language, which affects pronunciation, braille output, and automatic translation. This is one of the most commonly missed requirements in government document templates that are reused across departments without modification. Correction: Change to . --- ERROR 2: Non-descriptive page title WCAG criterion: 2.4.2 Page Titled — Level A The element contains only "Document" — a generic placeholder that provides no meaningful information. Users with multiple browser tabs open, screen reader users who navigate by page title, and search results all depend on a descriptive title to identify the content and its context. Correction: Replace with a descriptive title such as: <title>City of Maplewood Parks & Recreation — Annual Report FY2025, which identifies the organization, document type, and time period. --- ERROR 3: Document title and subtitle use styled divs instead of semantic headings WCAG criterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships — Level A The document title uses
and the subtitle uses
instead of semantic heading elements. These are visually styled to appear as headings but are announced by screen readers as plain text. Users cannot navigate by heading structure and the document hierarchy is not programmatically determinable. Correction: Replace
with

for the document title and
for the subtitle with

. Apply CSS styling directly to the semantic elements to maintain the same visual appearance. --- ERROR 4: Ambiguous link text WCAG criterion: 2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context) — Level A Two links use "click here" and "here" as their visible link text. When screen reader users navigate by links, they hear these labels in isolation with no indication of where each link leads. This is one of the most common link text failures on government websites. Correction: Replace with descriptive link text that identifies the destination: "read the full annual performance summary" and "view the complete FY2025 budget breakdown". --- ERROR 5: Department logo SVG missing accessibility attributes WCAG criterion: 1.1.1 Non-text Content — Level A The SVG logo has no role attribute, no aria-label, and no embedded element. Screen readers cannot identify the department or its branding from this image. Correction: Add role="img", a descriptive aria-label such as "City of Maplewood Parks and Recreation Department logo, featuring a stylized tree on a green background", and an embedded <title> element as the first child of the SVG. --- ERROR 6: Additional section headings use styled divs instead of semantic heading elements WCAG criterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships — Level A "Park Usage & Attendance" and "Capital Improvement Projects" use <div class="med-bold"> — visually styled as section headings but not programmatically identifiable as headings. The same fake heading pattern from Error 3 repeats throughout the document for all sections including "Facility Status Summary", "Program Enrollment Trends", and "Community Feedback & Next Steps". The "Completed Projects" label uses <div class="small-bold"> instead of a semantic subheading. Correction: Replace all <div class="med-bold"> section headings with <h2> elements and the <div class="small-bold"> subheading with <h3>. The document should have one <h1>, multiple <h2> sections, and <h3> for subsections. --- ERROR 7: Attendance data table built with CSS flexbox divs WCAG criterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships — Level A The six-row attendance comparison table is built with <div class="trow"> and <div class="tcell"> using CSS flexbox. There are no <table>, <th>, or <td> elements. Screen readers cannot identify this as a table, cannot announce its dimensions, and cannot associate header cells with data cells. This pattern is common in government documents created from templates using custom CSS grid layouts. Correction: Replace with a semantic HTML <table> using <thead> and <tbody>, <th scope="col"> for all column headers, and a visually hidden <caption> identifying the table as "Park Attendance Comparison — FY2024 vs FY2025". --- ERROR 8: Capital improvement projects listed using manual bullet divs WCAG criterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships — Level A Six project items are listed using <div class="list-item"> with manual bullet characters ("•"). Screen readers cannot identify these as a list, cannot announce the item count, and users cannot navigate using list keyboard shortcuts. Correction: Replace with a semantic <ul> containing <li> elements. Screen readers will then announce the list and its item count correctly. --- ERROR 9: Facility status conveyed by color only WCAG criterion: 1.4.1 Use of Color — Level A Facility status is conveyed exclusively through colored dots (green ●, orange ●, red ●). The explanatory paragraph above references "green", "orange", and "red" as the only way to understand each status meaning. Users with color vision deficiency cannot distinguish between the three status states. Correction: Replace colored dots with text badges that include both a background color and a descriptive text label, such as "Open", "Limited", and "Closed". Update the introductory paragraph to reference status labels rather than colors. --- ERROR 10: Facility status table built with CSS flexbox divs WCAG criterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships — Level A The facility status table uses the same div-based fake table pattern as Error 7. No semantic table structure exists and headers cannot be associated with data cells. Correction: Rebuild as a semantic HTML <table> with <thead>, <tbody>, <th scope="col"> column headers, and a visually hidden <caption> identifying it as "Current Operating Status of Major Facilities". --- ERROR 11: Bar chart has no text alternative, uses color-only bars, has low-contrast axis labels, and places the chart title inside SVG text WCAG criterion: 1.1.1 Non-text Content — Level A WCAG criterion: 1.4.1 Use of Color — Level A WCAG criterion: 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) — Level AA WCAG criterion: 1.4.5 Images of Text — Level AA The enrollment bar chart has no role, no aria-label, no <title>, and no <desc> element. All data encoded in the chart is invisible to screen readers. Six bars are differentiated by color alone with no patterns or direct value labels, making the chart uninterpretable for users with color vision deficiency. Axis label text uses color #999 on white at approximately 2.8:1 contrast, below the 4.5:1 minimum. The chart title is rendered as SVG text, which constitutes an image of text. Correction: Add role="img", a descriptive aria-label summarizing all enrollment values, and embedded <title> and <desc> elements. Provide an expandable data table via a <details> element. Apply unique fill patterns to each bar and add direct value labels above each bar. Move the chart title to an HTML <h3> element. Change all axis label text to #333. --- ERROR 12: Public comment deadline distinguished by color only WCAG criterion: 1.4.1 Use of Color — Level A The public comment deadline sentence is distinguished from surrounding paragraph text only by red color (color: #e74c3c). No bold weight, no text prefix, and no other non-color cue is provided. Users who cannot perceive color receive no visual indication that this text is a deadline notice. Correction: Wrap the sentence in a <strong> element to add bold weight and prepend a "Deadline:" text prefix. The red color may be retained as a supplementary visual cue but must not be the only indicator. --- ERROR 13: Contact bar links have insufficient contrast, no underlines, and the container lacks a navigation landmark WCAG criterion: 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) — Level AA WCAG criterion: 1.4.1 Use of Color — Level A WCAG criterion: 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value — Level A Link text in the contact bar uses color #4a8c4d on a #2c5f2e background, producing approximately 1.8:1 contrast — far below the 4.5:1 minimum. Green-on-green text is nearly invisible. Links also have text-decoration:none and are distinguished from the background only by this low-contrast color shift. The <div> container has no role or aria-label to identify it as a navigation region, preventing screen reader users from locating it via landmark navigation. Correction: Change link color to #d4edda, which achieves approximately 8.5:1 contrast on the #2c5f2e background. Add text-decoration:underline and visible focus styles. Wrap the container in a <nav aria-label="Department quick links"> element. --- ERROR 14: Footer text has insufficient contrast WCAG criterion: 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) — Level AA Footer text uses color #b0b0b0 on the page background, producing approximately 2.3:1 contrast. Contact information, address, and phone number in the footer are difficult or impossible to read for users with low vision. Correction: Change footer text color from #b0b0b0 to #555555, achieving approximately 7.0:1 contrast while retaining a visually lighter treatment than body text. <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <title>Document
City of Maplewood Parks & Recreation
Annual Report — Fiscal Year 2025

The Department of Parks & Recreation is proud to present our annual performance summary for FY2025. This report covers facility usage, program enrollment, capital improvements, and community feedback across all 14 city-owned parks and recreation centers. For more details, click here. You can also read the full budget breakdown here.

Park Usage & Attendance

Total park attendance for FY2025 reached 1.24 million visits, a 9% increase from the previous year. Riverside Park continues to be the most visited facility, accounting for 28% of all visits. Summer months (June–August) saw peak usage, with weekend attendance averaging 4,200 visitors per day across all facilities.

The department added 340 new programmed hours to the summer schedule, including expanded aquatics programming and a new nature education series at Oak Hollow Nature Center. Youth program enrollment grew by 15%, while senior program participation increased by 22%, reflecting the success of targeted outreach efforts.

Capital Improvement Projects

The department completed $3.8 million in capital improvements during FY2025. Major projects included the renovation of the Centennial Community Center gymnasium, installation of ADA-compliant playground equipment at three parks, and construction of a new multi-use trail segment connecting Riverside Park to the downtown greenway.

Completed Projects:
• Centennial Community Center gymnasium renovation ($1.2M)
• ADA playground installation at Riverside, Oak Hollow, and Lincoln parks ($680K)
• Riverside-to-Downtown trail connector, 1.8 miles ($940K)
• Westside Pool filtration system replacement ($320K)
• LED lighting upgrades at Memorial Athletic Complex ($440K)
• Oak Hollow Nature Center interpretive signage ($220K)

Community Feedback & Next Steps

The department conducted a community satisfaction survey in September 2025, receiving 2,847 responses. Overall satisfaction scored 4.3 out of 5.0, up from 4.1 in the prior year. The most requested improvements were extended evening hours at community centers, additional pickleball courts, and improved wayfinding signage.

Based on community input, the department is developing its FY2026 strategic plan. Public comment on the draft plan closes December 15, 2025 and can be submitted through the city website or at any community center front desk. The Parks Advisory Board will review all comments at their January meeting.

Four route lines are differentiated by color alone with no variation in line style or route labels on the map. Legend text uses color #999 on white at approximately 2.8:1 contrast, below the 4.5:1 minimum. Correction: Add role="img", a descriptive aria-label summarizing all routes and stops, and embedded <title> and <desc> elements. Add route labels directly on the map lines. Apply distinct line styles to each route (solid, dashed, dotted, and solid with markers). Update legend text to #333. Provide an expandable text list of route descriptions via a <details> element. --- ERROR 12: Fare table built with CSS flexbox divs WCAG criterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships — Level A The fare schedule is built with the same div-based fake table pattern as the route schedule. Five rider categories and three fare types have no programmatic header associations. The red color may be retained as a supplementary visual cue. --- ERROR 14: Accessibility accommodations listed using manual bullet divs WCAG criterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships — Level A Six accommodation items are listed using <div class="list-item"> with manual bullet characters ("•"). Screen readers cannot identify these as a list, cannot announce the item count, and users cannot navigate using list keyboard shortcuts. Correction: Replace with a semantic <ul> containing <li> elements. --- ERROR 15: Service alert has insufficient text contrast, no alert role, and the footer has insufficient contrast, missing link underlines, and is non-semantic WCAG criterion: 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) — Level AA WCAG criterion: 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value — Level A WCAG criterion: 1.4.1 Use of Color — Level A The service alert text uses color #c9a800 on the #fff3cd background at approximately 2.1:1 contrast — critical service disruption information is barely readable. The alert container is a plain <div> with no role="alert", so assistive technology does not treat it as time-sensitive content. The footer uses color #bbb on white at approximately 2.3:1 contrast for both body text and links, failing the 4.5:1 minimum. Footer links have no underlines, relying on color alone to indicate interactivity. The footer container is a <div> rather than a semantic <footer> element. Correction: Change alert text to #555 on #fff3cd to achieve approximately 6.4:1 contrast. Add role="alert" to the alert container. Rider's Guide — Effective January 2026

Welcome to the Maplewood Transit Authority rider's guide. This document provides essential information about routes, schedules, fares, and accessibility services. To plan your trip, click here. For service alerts and detours, click here. To provide feedback on your ride experience, click here.

Route Overview

The Maplewood Transit Authority operates 12 fixed routes serving the city and surrounding communities. Routes are categorized as Express, Local, or Circulator services. Express routes provide limited-stop service along major corridors, Local routes serve all designated stops, and Circulator routes connect key destinations within specific districts.

Route types are identified as follows: RED for express routes, BLUE for local routes, and GREEN for circulator routes. Look for these color indicators on all route signage and printed schedules.

Accessibility Services

First-Time Homebuyer Down Payment Assistance Program
City of Maplewood — Department of Housing & Community Development

The City of Maplewood offers down payment assistance of up to $25,000 for eligible first-time homebuyers purchasing within city limits. This program is funded through federal Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) and HOME Investment Partnership funds. To check if you qualify, click here. To download the application, click here.

Quick Links

Program Eligibility Requirements

To qualify for the First-Time Homebuyer Down Payment Assistance Program, applicants must meet all of the following criteria. Household income must fall at or below 80% of the Area Median Income (AMI) as published annually by HUD. The property must be located within Maplewood city limits and must pass a housing quality standards inspection.

Applicant Requirements:
1. Must be a first-time homebuyer (no ownership interest in any property in the past 3 years)
2. Must complete an 8-hour HUD-approved homebuyer education course
3. Must obtain mortgage pre-approval from a participating lender
4. Must contribute a minimum of $1,500 in personal funds toward closing
5. Must occupy the home as primary residence for a minimum of 5 years
6. Must not have any outstanding federal debts or tax liens

Employee Benefits Overview

Full-time City of Maplewood employees receive a comprehensive benefits package. Benefits are effective on the first day of the month following 30 days of employment.

Health Insurance
Medical, dental, vision for employee + family. City covers 85% of premium.
Paid Time Off
12 vacation days, 12 sick days, 11 paid holidays per year. Increases with tenure.
Retirement
IMRF pension. City matches 457(b) contributions up to 3% of salary.
Tuition Assistance
Up to $5,250/year for approved degree programs. Must maintain 3.0 GPA.

Hiring Process

The city's hiring process typically takes 4–6 weeks from application to offer. Below is an overview of the steps.

  1. Apply Online — Submit your application through the city's careers portal.
  2. Screening & Review — HR reviews applications against minimum qualifications.
  3. Interview — Qualified candidates are invited to interview with the hiring department.
  4. Background Check — A background check is conducted for the selected finalist.
  5. Offer & Onboarding — A conditional offer is extended and onboarding begins.

The city is actively expanding its workforce to support growing community needs. New for FY2026: Seven new positions have been added for FY2026, including roles in IT, Public Safety, and Community Development.

Required Application Materials:

  • Completed city employment application (online or PDF)
  • Current resume
  • Cover letter addressing position qualifications
  • Three professional references with contact information
  • Copies of relevant licenses or certifications (if applicable)

Adoption Requirements

To adopt an animal from Maplewood Animal Services, applicants must meet the following requirements:

  • Must be 18 years of age or older
  • Valid government-issued photo ID required
  • Proof of current address (utility bill or lease agreement)
  • Landlord permission letter if renting (where applicable)
  • All current household pets must be up to date on vaccinations

FY2025 Water Quality Summary

The City of Maplewood's water supply met all federal and state drinking water standards in FY2025. This report summarizes test results from 847 samples collected across 12 monitoring stations throughout the distribution system. Click here to download the full technical report or click here to view our testing methodology.

All primary contaminant levels within EPA limits. No drinking water advisories are currently in effect for Maplewood residents.
Precautionary Notice: Residents with lead service lines built before 1986 are encouraged to run cold water for 2 minutes before use. Learn more.

Services & Programs Overview

The City of Maplewood Senior Services Department provides a comprehensive range of programs and support services for residents aged 60 and older. Download the full senior resource directory or request a printed copy of the resource guide by mail.

Notice: New enrollment period for the Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP) opens April 1, 2026. Learn more about LIHEAP enrollment for 2026.
In-Home Support Services
Eligibility: Age 60+ · Income-based sliding scale
Personal care, homemaking assistance, and meal preparation for seniors who need support to remain living independently at home. Services coordinated through the Maplewood Area Agency on Aging.
Learn more about In-Home Support Services
Senior Transportation Program NEW
Eligibility: Age 65+ or disability · No income requirement
Door-to-door transportation for medical appointments, grocery shopping, and senior center activities. Advance scheduling required. Service area covers all of Maplewood and surrounding communities.
Learn more about the Senior Transportation Program
Benefits Enrollment Assistance
Eligibility: All seniors age 60+
Free assistance enrolling in Medicare, Medicaid, SNAP, utility assistance programs, and property tax exemptions. Appointments available at the Senior Center or by phone.
Learn more about Benefits Enrollment Assistance

Benefit Programs at a Glance

Property Tax Freeze
Available to homeowners 65+ with income under $65,000. Freezes assessed value.
Prescription Assistance
State SeniorCare program covers generic medications for eligible seniors.
Meals on Wheels
Home-delivered meals 5 days per week. Sliding scale cost. Waitlist currently open.
Telephone Reassurance
Daily wellness check-in calls for isolated seniors. Volunteer-staffed program.
